text: This is a video of a patient with a perforated peptic ulcer disease.
This patient presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain and she had a chest x-ray that showed the free air under the diaphragm.
I typically approach these patients laparoscopically and do not perform a CT of the abdomen pelvis.
After resuscitation, as long as they are not hypertensive, they'll have a diagnostic laparoscopy.
And I always can send these patients and inform them that they might have a persistent leak.

Following the procedure, I typically place all these patients in the French position with a little bit of a reverse Trendelenburg with the surgeon standing between the patient's legs. and the camera at the umbilical port.
The surgeon inserts an additional 5mm trocar to retract the gallbladder, with the camera operator assisting in holding the gallbladder for better visualization during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, potentially followed by irrigation and additional suction, before proceeding to repair the perforation.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture to repair the perforation, closing it primarily with interrupted sutures.
And I just like using a silk because it's easier to suture with.

Of note, I'd like to say that those patients that were just suctioned without irrigation had a significant shorter hospital stay due to lack of ileus. But my sample is too small to make any scientific conclusion.
Those that were suctioned followed by copious irrigation and suctioning had longer ileus.
So after a primary repair, I always do a leak test by asking anesthesia to connect the NG tube to two liters of oxygen.
After confirming the absence of a leak, the surgeon begins applying a patch over the repaired ulcer site.
The surgeon places three sutures to secure the patch over the primary closure site, using a sequence of silk, vicryl, and silk sutures to optimize the coverage of the omentum.
Though this could be told very easily laparoscopically by pulling the stitch and looking at the other end. But working with the resin, it made it very easy to avoid confusing the sutures.
After that, I usually place it to seal. There's no scientific data behind this, but I still like using it on top of the repair.
The surgeon places three sutures and then elevates the omentum to cover the repaired area. In the absence of omentum, the falciform ligament is used as an alternative to cover the repair.

The surgeon ties down the omentum over the repair site, adjusting the patient's position slightly off Trendelenburg to prevent the transverse colon from descending. The omentum is lifted and sutured without cutting, securing it over the repaired area.
And here you can see, we can very easily tell what the next suture is. And it's typically tied down in the usual fashion.

Also of note in these patients, I don't place any drains. And they typically have an NG tube post-operatively, which is removing post-op day number one. Once I see a bile in the NG tube, which is almost always on post-op day number one. And I also do not perform an upper GI study on these patients.

Post-op day number one, these patients are started immediately on a full liquid diet and on treatment for H. pylori eradication. And about six weeks after the Six weeks after the surgery, they'll get an EGD with a biopsy and also confirmation for H. pylori eradication. Though most of the time, I do not know if H. pylori infection in the first place. Thank you.
